reasoning: The question asks if at least one European country or the EU will commit to providing HIMARS-compatible systems or substitutes by the end of 2025. Historically, European countries (like the UK, Germany, and France) have consistently provided advanced weapons systems (e.g., M270, MARS II, LRU) and upgraded their commitments when U.S. aid was delayed or when Ukraine faced critical gaps. As of 2024/2025, several European nations are rapidly acquiring alternative systems like the Israeli PULS and South Korean K239 Chunmoo. Poland, for instance, has ordered hundreds of Chunmoos. With the U.S. share of rocket artillery being extremely high and Europe under pressure to bear more of the assistance burden, there is a strong incentive to either donate older M270s, allocate EU funds (like the proposed €40 billion package) to procure new MLRS substitutes for Ukraine, or pledge a subset of newly acquired systems. The threshold for a 'Yes' is low—requiring just one European country to pledge any number of systems. However, Europe's own depleted stockpiles and the slow delivery of new PULS/Chunmoo orders present constraints. Balancing these factors, it is quite likely that at least one such commitment will be made by late 2025.
